speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three of the interview with Elizabeth Etinger, on October 11, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would like to go back to explain why I had to-- to get them believe that I'm not a Jew, I'm Russian. They told me to repeat after them, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN] Because they believe every one Jew doesn't pronounce [RUSSIAN] right, soft, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. And they thought this, if I repeat and I couldn't say [RUSSIAN] that will prove that I'm a Jew. But because I-- I was-- how to say-- speak Russian very clearly, and [RUSSIAN] wasn't a problem to me to say the right way. So, I suppose that saved me too. So that's why I wanted to explain why I said so. Because to sort of let them believe that I'm not Jew, but I'm Russian. I said that they gave me a sentence, life sentence, life in concentration camp. They sent me to-- they send me to prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prison--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long? How long?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a month in the prison. And then they send me to a concentration camp in Russia, Vodokachka. It's not far from Nikolaev.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about the time that you were in the prison itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In prison, the only thing I remember, it was a very bad experience that of course, we didn't have much to eat. And we haven't got much to drink. But there was a prisoner, which have a sickness, we call it black epilepsy. And we had it two or three times a day. And we all were-- it was very frightening. And of course, we wanted to help her. And she-- she was shaking. It was a terrible experience. And a lot of them-- who was-- I was arrested as political. And there was some of them killers and all sort of prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed there only for a month, and then they sent me to concentration camp. In concentration camp-- What month was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was approximately a month after gestapo, I presume--

speaker: interviewer
text: July.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that would have been about July. And in concentration camp, in Vodokachka--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us where it's near?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's near Nikolaev, where I was arrested, not far, anyway. In concentration, there was a plantation of tobacco-- tobacco plantation. So what was our job? We would-- and there was a plantation of cabbages. What I-- what we were doing is there is no watering. It was we have to take the water from the well, and in buckets, and take the buckets, and go and put the water over the cabbages, and tobacco-- tobacco plants, and all sort of things. All day we were going and doing this. But later on the when the leaves were ready to pick, so we pick up the leaves and put on the strings, and let them dry. That's where I started to smoke.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we didn't have much heat. And just to kill my hunger, because I was young. I was only 22. So the hunger was killing people. So I started-- what I did, it has to be done very carefully so that nobody sees, just [INAUDIBLE]. That was Russian, really, not Germans. But the Russian, but they were worse than Germans. They-- they look like a hawk that nobody will pinch anything all day. But I manage sometimes to pinch dry leaf. And I crush it. I put in a newspaper, and I smoke. And it helped me to kill hunger. And I wasn't hungry that much. So-- and I smoke till now. And this concentration camp--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 61 people. It was a mixed concentration camp. There was man and woman. And the-- the kapos or the [NON-ENGLISH] mostly were Cossack-- Ukrainian. They was looking after us. So we had a very bad time. Because we were left with no food sometimes. And if they felt like it, they give us something. If they felt not, then we didn't get nothing. But we were working all day from the morning till the night. And then we were glad to come and just-- we were in barracks that have straw on there. And we were put on this straw. And we slept, because we very tired. And it was a blessing. Because then we were not hungry too. That-- that lasts for six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes, yes. But we were lucky, because we were dealing with water. We were taking the water to the plants and things like this. So we could always get a drink of water. So that was a help too. So the food wasn't that important.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about sanitary conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, don't ask. Sanitary conditions? There was no sanitary conditions. Who got sick, they straight away took them away and killed them. And that's it. They wouldn't bother with that. You have to be strong, and-- and really healthy to get through it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And toilet facilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Toilet facilities was outside the doors. It-- it was a hole, with these two walls, and that's where you do it. On the-- on the ground, nearly. You see, facilities in Russia was never good, particularly in concentration camp. It was-- it was temporary. It never was concentration, temporary German, and-- and they did it the cheapest way they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's it. And to get sick and be killed was very easy. We lived from day to day. Today we live. Tomorrow we don't know. That's how it was. But we were happy that we were alive, whatever, wherever. Were you close to anyone in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that camp, I-- not particularly. I was frightened. Because it was in the territory of Ukraine. I knew Ukraine always were antisemites. Nobody they knew that I a Jew. But I don't trust them with nothing. And I was frightened to get friendly with anybody. I can say something not about my personal, but something what I think, and somebody will go and-- you see from then, from the arresting me and putting me in gestapo hands, I was very, very careful. I practically didn't trust nobody, nobody. I was frightened. Because I trust this girl. I will never, ever if somebody said that she can give you away, I would have spit. I wouldn't believe it. But that's what happened. And since then there is that I learned a very good lesson. Don't trust nobody. And that's how I lived for a long, long time. I didn't trust nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after Vodokachka?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed there for six months. It was in December or January. Now that was snow. It was already winter, not even autumn, but winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: End of October '43?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's about October, maybe November. I was sent to first, that's not only me, 61 women and men were separated. And we were sent to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train, like a cattle train. In one of those-- it was straw. We would stay in the straw. There was very bad conditions. We have to clean after ourselves, and to throw away the-- the-- if somebody vomited or somebody this, we have to clean after them and after yourself. We had a bucket where we did our business. And that was thrown out when we could, and when it was permitted. It was all command. What they commanded, we did it, and that was that. We were going through Russia and to Germany. Buchenwald was in Weimar. It was-- the journey was pretty long, I would say. But I can't tell you exactly how long. I know that we were in Buchenwald in winter. It was most probably-- most probably-- January, maybe beginning of February. That long last this. Because if I don't mistake, it was in January or February. I'm not sure, we come to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: You thought--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my, pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You previously thought that you had arrived in Buchenwald in November '43.

speaker: interviewee
text: In November, maybe in November. You see, I-- most probably in November. I know that only one thing, when we come to Buchenwald, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they stopped-- Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you just describe Buchenwald, did you ever get off the train at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, not off the train. They opened the door to have a fresh air, but never.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for weeks, you didn't get off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the way to the-- to-- to Buchenwald we didn't get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was three to four weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's-- they only opened the door, we stopped. When we stop, they opened the door to give a bit of fresh air.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in each wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: In each, we have-- oh, about 20, 25. Because we have two wagon. No, it will be more, 30 and 30. Because we have two wagon of women's. And two wagons with for the men's. So I suppose it was about 30, 31 person in each of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have room to sit or lie?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the-- that's the straw. Wherever you sit, you lie. There was no special for laying or sitting. Yes, so that was your place. You sit, and you lie in the same place. OK. So what else am I to tell you?

speaker: interviewer
text: What food did you get to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food, they was bringing us, in the morning we had coffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a little piece of bread that much. And at midday, we had soup, soup that you can give only a pig to eat. But we ate it. We were happy to get that. And that was all the time during the whole concentration camp, except Buchenwald, we had soup and coffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this was on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was on the train too, the same thing. After that--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see when you arrived at Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: We see this, in Buchenwald, when we arrived in Buchenwald, first of all, we saw a lot of German shepherds, a lot of dogs, with those-- German soldiers. And they-- they was calling SS men, SS, and woman SS. And they come to everyone, two, three, or four of them with a dog. Opened the door, and set, out. Raus, raus, we knew, out. We go. We went out. And they took us all in one-- there was-- I remember we had to go through a big-- not fence, but door-- not door.

speaker: interviewer
text: Arch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: An arch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Arch, gate, a big gate. And we went through this gate. And they took us all in a room, the women separate, men separate. So we women went to one room. [SIGHS] They told us to take off everything. And there was a big basin, or what, over the ground, a big one, like this high. And they told us to jump, take off everything, and to jump there. We all jumped. It was white water. I found it was sort of disinfection to take the things that you might have brought there. And from there on, they-- they give us some new dresses. They took away all we had. We have to get some new-- new or old, I don't know. But they give us something to dress in. And they took us in one block. We all-- all 61 of them in one block were.

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't in uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that was a downstairs in a cellar. But it was a big room. There was tables, and there was little rooms where it was the beds for us. After all what-- what we went through, we were really happy to be there. We were looked after-- after us, they looked very good. They give us very good food. They give us to sleep on real bed, sometimes two of them. But we had a bed--

speaker: interviewer
text: They weren't wooden bunks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually a bed. And-- later on, we learn why. I learned. Because I was the one that spoke German, and I could communicate. Apparently in Buchenwald, was a lot of Communists, who was taken to concentration when just a year after '33, when Hitler come to-- to the-- Reich, or whatever it is called. And he, of course, all Communists and even their children he put in concentration camp. So there was a lot of them. And of course when they heard that they come, prisoners from Russia, they were glad to help us with everything they've got, to the point that they-- we have food from the German officer-- officer's kitchen. We couldn't believe it. But apparently they have underground-- they even come tonight-- every night, and tell us news, what's going on, on the front, what's going on with the Red Army and the Germans. That then Russian start to sort of-- start to push Germans out of Russian territories. So we knew the news. But it was far from the end of the war still. But it was such a happy occasion to hear that Russians is going forward.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so this treatment was given by German--

speaker: interviewee
text: German--

speaker: interviewer
text: Communists.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Communists who was in concentration camp. Because in concentration camp, was all sort of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How can--

speaker: interviewee
text: But all sort of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you explain how they could get you the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I'm going to explain. There was a lot of people arrested. There was German Jews, which stayed there too. They taking away everything they possessed. They-- and they was still staying there and sending-- Germans kill a lot of German Jews. But still the rich ones managed to stay alive, you know, and most of them stay in Buchenwald. Why? In every position, every-- every work that has to be done in concentration camp was done by prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the kitchen was working prisoners. The longer you stay in-- in concentration camp, the more you have sort of chances to get a good job. Do you understand? Because Germans looked at it, oh, we will rehabilitate them. We-- we will make them believe in us. So we learn-- we teach them to be as we are. So that's Communism.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met a boy. He was arrested when he was 16 years of age. And when I met him he's 28. So this boy, which was arrested so young, but was so long in concentration camp, he was the head of the underground. He brought us the news. And he make sure that we have good food from there. As I said, everything was done by the prisoners. Who were-- a German was in the headquarters, they was in the offices. They was commanding. But the work was done by prisoners. That's why they could organize things. They were very happy to have us. Oh, from Russia come prisoner. That we have to help them. And they helped us. The best memories, the whole us have from we were not long there, because they send us right away to Buchenwald-- to Ravensbruck. But the best memories we had were from Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were there about maybe four or six weeks. I'm not sure how long. But we were not long there. But, they're organized-- like, I have to tell you. Because they teach me how to have underground in concentration camp. You see, they said what to do, and how to do it. So I learned a lot from them again, how to-- behave in concentration camp. What to do in concentration camp, when I will go out of Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can they-- can you give us some examples?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I will tell you some. They have prepared what's going to be in concentration camp. They said to me, you-- you have to be strong, otherwise you will-- you will not survive. Because where you go, you will be very bad treated, very little given, and you have to learn to survive. To survive you have to have somebody that gives you a push. If you seek to make sure that it will be done very discreet, to put her to the hospital, and tell, oh, she have a cold or she have a-- so that she can be cured, and go back to work. So little things-- you will be searched. Remember, whatever we give you now could be taken from you. You have to know how to put it away so that they can't catch you. So those little things was a help later on for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say--

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, because they didn't know Russian, but they-- they know how to speak in German, and I knew. So the most information they gave to me. And I explained the others. You see, I learned a lot. What I can think now, was-- was very, very-- yeah. There was a point that we knew that we're going to be separated from the men. In our group of women, there was husbands. There were sisters and brothers, which were in the same position. But they were men, and we were women.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we couldn't stay-- they couldn't see them. So they come to me and said, Liza, please, ask them before we go to see our relatives. So that we can say goodbye each other. And I did it. I did it. How did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I ask-- ask this-- the leader. I told him, look we have prisoners here who've got brother and husbands in the camp that come from Russia now. And they would like to say goodbye. We didn't do nothing. They did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German Communists. They did it. They make sure that every one woman that had a man saw them. So every day, at night, mostly at night, he organized that. Today he said, you can see your brother. Tomorrow, you will see your husband or you see your father, or you see your mother. That's how everybody who had somebody in the men's quarters was they organized so that before we left Buchenwald, they see each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, for example, I remember like this. I had a-- a-- a terrible toothache. And I told him, what can I do? In concentration camp you can't have toothache. So they have a-- a dentist there. And he took me to dentist. And dentist put fillings in my tooth. So you see what I'm-- what I'm saying-- that the best-- the best thing was for us Buchenwald after this whole horror that we got through in Russia, coming to Buchenwald was really this. But-- Did you see other groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not for-- no, we couldn't. That was the one thing. They wouldn't let us to mix, even to the dentist. I had to go with somebody. Because they were frightened too we speak. So when I went to dentist, chap from this organization, he come with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I told you, the Communist have underground organization there. From this underground, they-- they wouldn't let us to mix with other prisoners. We were very separated. The men, later on, was-- I don't know how they were treated. But they was left in Buchenwald. We were sent to Ravensbruck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so, you said that you never mixed. You never saw any other prisoners at Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It-- it was all-- all hush-hush. Like whatever they did for us, it was hush-hush. And to make sure it's hush-hush, we couldn't mix with other prisoners. They knew we are going to Ravensbruck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you active within the underground as an individual in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, in Buchenwald? No, no. No, nothing because I just came. But I was lucky, lucky to-- to meet them. You know, because they organized this all. I suppose we wouldn't have this all if they didn't organize. Because they were so happy to meet Russian prisoners, particularly-- particularly political. Because we were all political prisoners, Russian political prisoners, not others, but political.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, that's what-- that was the happy days to be in prison, but in like prison like this. That was something out of this world. Because we never thought that we would have this. You see? But it was short-lived, of course. And we were sent to Buchenwald.
